dopamine-detox.subtitle: Break digital addiction in 30 days
dopamine-detox.description: Step out of phones, social media, and fast dopamine. Reset your brain.

1.title: The Science of Addiction
1.teaching:
Dopamine isn't a reward chemical — it's an anticipation chemical. Your brain releases dopamine while imagining a future reward, not when the reward arrives.

While scrolling your phone, every video, every notification is a small dopamine hit. Your brain learns: 'Phone = reward exists.' Result: constantly checking your phone, reaching for it when bored, real life feeling 'flat.'

Good news: this learning is reversible. Your brain settles into a new baseline in 7–30 days. You're starting now.
1.action: Every time you open your phone today, wait 10 seconds before looking at the screen. Just wait. Then do what you wanted.
1.reflectionPrompt: Did you notice how many times you opened your phone 'without thinking' today?

2.title: Kill Notifications
2.teaching:
Every notification creates a micro-explosion in your attention. Research: it takes 23 minutes to fully refocus after one notification.

If you get 50 notifications a day, you're never fully focused. Your brain is in constant 'is something incoming?' mode.

Notifications don't serve you — they serve apps. Their growth metrics. You pay the cost.
2.action: Turn OFF every notification on your phone. Keep only calls and direct messages. Do this now. If you postpone, you won't.
2.reflectionPrompt: Which notification was hardest to disable? Why?

3.title: Social Media Fast
3.teaching:
Social media is one of the fastest dopamine sources. Infinite scroll, unpredictable content, social comparison — you've plugged your brain into an optimized exploitation machine.

Today you start a test: 24 hours, no social media. Instagram, TikTok, Twitter, YouTube Shorts — all of it.

The first few hours will be hard. You'll auto-unlock your phone and reach for the app. This is 'phantom reaching.' Normal. It passes.
3.action: Remove all social media apps from your home screen. Put them in a folder named 'Enemies.' Don't open any of them for 24 hours.
3.reflectionPrompt: When did the urge 'just one quick check' come up? What were you feeling at that moment?

4.title: Cut Junk Content
4.teaching:
Junk content = empty calories for your brain. Random YouTube videos, Reddit scrolling, news refreshing, podcasts only as background.

What you don't notice: this content isn't just making you dumber — it's making you an accessory. Your brain gets used to consuming others' thoughts and forgets how to produce its own.

Today we install a filter: every piece of content you consume must serve a purpose.
4.action: Don't consume any content today. Only produce. Write, draw, walk and think, have a real conversation. No information consumption today.
4.reflectionPrompt: How many times today did you feel 'information hunger'? What was that hunger really for?

5.title: Allow Boredom
5.teaching:
Modern life is programmed to fill every empty moment. Phone in the elevator. Phone in the bathroom. Headphones while walking. Video while eating.

Result: your brain never enters idle mode. Creativity is born in idle mode. Solutions come in idle mode. Self-knowledge happens in idle mode.

Boredom isn't a sickness — it's a signal. Your brain saying: 'I need empty time.' Listen to it.
5.action: Do nothing for 30 minutes today. No phone, no music, no book. Just sit or walk. Observe your thoughts. If you get bored, great — keep going.
5.reflectionPrompt: What thought emerged in your mind by the end of 30 minutes? Were you aware of it before?
